title: Object Tracking Basics
concept: Object tracking involves assigning a unique ID to each detected object and following it across video frames.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main purpose of feature aggregation in video networks?

options.text: To smooth detections and reduce bounding box flicker
To double the framerate of the input clip
To convert color features into spatial coordinates

exercises.explain: Yes! Aggregating features over time stabilizes bounding boxes and prevents jitter.

cards.title: Who is who?
cards.body: Imagine counting people entering a store. A detector tells you: 'there are 3 people in Frame 1' and '3 people in Frame 2.' But are they the same people? Did someone leave and someone else enter? To answer this, we need to track individual identities!

cards.title: Object Tracking
cards.body: Object Tracking is the process of identifying unique objects in a video and tracking their paths (trajectories). The tracker assigns a unique **ID** (e.g. ID #1, ID #2) to each object and maintains that ID across all frames.

cards.title: Nametags on runners
cards.body: A detector is like a photographer taking snapshots of a marathon. A tracker is like sticking a unique bib number on each runner's chest. No matter how many photos you take, you can follow runner #42's journey throughout the entire race.

cards.title: Multi-Object Tracking (MOT)
cards.body: In sports analytics, trackers follow 22 players and a ball. By assigning unique IDs, the system calculates statistics like running distance, speed, and player heatmaps for each individual athlete.

cards.title: Detection vs. Tracking
cards.body: Don't confuse detection with tracking! Bounding box detection locates objects in a single frame. Tracking links those locations across frames. A system can track objects using simple physics (like speed and direction) even if the detector misses a frame.

cards.title: Detection only vs. Detection + Tracking
cards.body: Comparing coordinate detection and continuous tracking outputs:

left.text:
Frame 1: Pedestrian
Frame 2: Pedestrian
No link between identities

right.text:
Frame 1: Pedestrian (ID #1)
Frame 2: Pedestrian (ID #1)
Maintains identity and path

exercises.prompt: What is the primary goal of object tracking?

options.text: To follow unique objects across frames and assign them persistent IDs
To classify the breed of a dog in a static photo
To increase the frame rate of the video
To compress video files into smaller formats

exercises.explain: Correct! Tracking links detections across frames to maintain unique object identities (IDs).

exercises.prompt: True or false: An object tracker can only work if the object detector never misses a single frame.
exercises.explain: False. Advanced trackers use motion models (like speed estimation) to predict where an object should be, even if the detector misses it for a few frames.

exercises.sentence: While detection locates objects, tracking follows their ___ over time.
exercises.explain: Tracking traces the paths or trajectories of unique objects as they move across frames.

exercises.prompt: Which of these outputs indicates that a tracker is active?
exercises.explain: The presence of a persistent identification number (ID #7) indicates the object is being tracked.

exercises.prompt: You are building a system to count how many unique visitors enter a shopping mall. What happens if you only use a detector without a tracker?

options.text: You will overcount massively, because a single person walking around will be counted as a new person in every frame.
You will count zero people, because detectors cannot locate stationary visitors.
The camera feed will freeze because detection is too slow.
The visitor count will be stored in the color channels.

exercises.explain: Exactly! A detector simply counts boxes in the current frame. If one person stands in front of the camera for 100 frames, the detector sees '1 person' 100 times. A tracker links them, noting it is the SAME ID #1 visitor. Next: let's study deep learning-based tracking!
